one_line_takeaway: DCP proposes protocol-level (not application-level) safety primitives -- capability tokens, range/type/unit checks, and dry-run -- for LLM-driven MCU control, and its own adversarial study against five real LLMs shows these primitives close the capability-escalation gap completely (100% vs 0% for MCP-family protocols) and the prompt-injection gap substantially (78% vs ~1%) at no measurable latency cost, though the paper transparently discloses that reaching that prompt-injection number required a mid-study patch after an initial 30% showing.
key_finding: DCP rejects 100% of capability-escalation attempts (all 138 'reboot the lamp' calls across the five LLMs) versus 0% for Raw MCP and IoT-MCP, which the authors attribute to those protocols having no caller-capability concept at the schema layer; OpenAPI's OAuth2 scopes also reject all 138. On prompt injection, DCP rejects 78% of 160 parsed calls (125/160) after a mid-study spec revision -- initially DCP's Param type lacked string pattern/max_length fields and scored only 30% on a 295-call sub-corpus, prompting the authors to add these fields (v0.3.1, ~12 lines across manifest.py and safety.py plus five tests) and re-run the same corpus, after which DCP tied OpenAPI's pattern-based rejection. MCP-family protocols (Raw MCP, IoT-MCP) caught only 1-2 of these calls (~1%), purely as a side effect of additionalProperties:false on an empty-schema tool. Out-of-range and unit-confusion categories showed modest protocol-layer rejection rates (DCP and OpenAPI both at 11%/6% per the abstract's headline figures, with per-category chart values of 8/7/5/27/100/78 reported for DCP across the six categories in Figure 3) because, per the authors' own analysis, LLMs frequently self-correct obvious numeric errors before emitting the tool call. On latency, DCP's validated wire path measured 15.60 ms median round-trip on ESP32-S3 versus 15.59 ms for an IoT-MCP-style newline-JSON wire on identical hardware -- a 4-microsecond difference the authors describe as within the ~0.4 ms standard deviation of either run, i.e. no measurable safety-check overhead. Firmware footprint: the DCP layer alone measured 27.6 KB flash / 0.6 KB RAM on ESP32 (via differencing against a bare Arduino sketch), versus IoT-MCP's reported 74 KB peak memory (a cited external number, not independently reproduced by this paper) -- roughly two orders of magnitude smaller on RAM. The 27.6 KB flash figure exceeds the paper's own original <16 KB design target, which the authors explicitly acknowledge predates always-linked on-device HMAC-SHA256 verification, and they revise the target rather than hide the miss.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Device Context Protocol (DCP), a compact wire protocol and manifest schema for letting LLMs invoke tools/intents on resource-constrained microcontrollers (MCUs), positioned as the missing layer between MCP (which the authors argue is moving toward enterprise SaaS connectivity, not embedded devices) and physical hardware. The protocol's own design contributions are: (1) a 6-byte fixed header + CBOR payload wire frame (19 bytes typical, 35 with an optional 16-byte truncated HMAC-SHA256), transport-agnostic across UART/COBS+CRC, MQTT, BLE GATT, and USB-CDC; (2) a YAML manifest schema in which units-as-types, first-class parameter ranges, and per-intent capability strings (e.g. lamp.write) are protocol-layer primitives rather than application code; (3) a host-side 'Bridge' as the sole trust boundary that issues/verifies HMAC-SHA256-signed, scoped, time-limited capability tokens and performs manifest-driven type/range validation and dry-run evaluation (kind=0x81) before any frame reaches the device; (4) reference implementations -- a ~1800-SLOC asyncio Python Bridge with five transports (loopback, UART, MQTT, BLE, and an MCP-server wrapper) and an Arduino-compatible ESP32 C++ firmware with self-contained CBOR-subset and SHA-256/HMAC implementations; (5) a language-neutral YAML conformance suite of golden frames for multi-author interoperability; and (6) an empirical adversarial study of five real LLMs against DCP versus Raw MCP, IoT-MCP, and OpenAPI. The reference implementation is MIT-licensed on GitHub (device-context-protocol/dcp) with the Python Bridge on PyPI as pydcp.
threat_model: The paper's threat model is an 'unreliable caller': an LLM tool-invocation client that may hallucinate arguments (out-of-range values, wrong types, unknown intents), suffer unit confusion, or be redirected by indirect/direct prompt injection into issuing unauthorized or destructive device commands (e.g. capability escalation to reboot a device, or exfiltration/DoS via oversized label payloads). The device itself is treated as passive and untrusted to defend itself in v0.x -- devices do not verify capability tokens by default and assume any frame reaching them was pre-authorized by the Bridge (the Bridge is explicitly named 'the sole trust boundary'); per-frame device-side HMAC verification is supported for shared physical media (RS-485 multidrop, public MQTT) but not required. The paper explicitly does NOT address mesh routing, multi-device atomic transactions, fine-grained per-intent/per-parameter rate limiting, or a downgrade-attack-resistant in-band signing marker (the authors note the absence of an in-band signed/unsigned marker is intentional, to prevent a downgrade attack, at the cost of requiring out-of-band configuration discipline between Bridge and device).
limitations: The authors' own 'What this paper does not prove' section (Section 6.1) states: only one MCU (ESP32-WROOM-32) was runtime-validated over one transport (UART/USB-CDC); the broader ESP32 family (C3, C6, H2, P4, S3, ESP8266) is build-validated only, not bench-validated, and non-ESP32 architectures used by IoT-MCP (Cortex-M0+, nRF52840) are entirely untested. Full end-to-end latency including the actual LLM API round-trip was not measured -- only the host-to-device wire leg was benchmarked, so the paper explicitly does not compare against IoT-MCP's reported ~205 ms end-to-end figure. The adversarial LLM study covers only five open/mid-tier models from four vendors (DeepSeek, Alibaba, Zhipu, MiniMax) served via SiliconFlow; closed-source frontier models (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini) were not tested, and integration with AgentDojo's full task suites (banking, Slack, travel, workspace) is left to future work. The paper transparently reports that its own protocol initially failed on prompt injection (30% rejection) before a targeted patch (v0.3.1) closed the gap to 78% -- this is disclosed as the study's actual value (exposing a real spec gap) rather than concealed. Design limitations acknowledged by the authors: no in-band signed/unsigned marker (intentional, to avoid downgrade attacks, but requires out-of-band configuration discipline); no mesh routing or multi-device atomic transactions (deferred to v0.4+); capability tokens are coarse-grained, not bound to specific intents, parameters, or rates. Devices do not verify capability tokens themselves by default in v0.x, making the Bridge a single point of trust/failure by design -- a deliberate trade-off the authors name explicitly (trading defense-in-depth for tractability on cheap silicon) but do not further stress-test (e.g. no evaluation of what happens if the Bridge itself is compromised).

datasets_used: Self-generated corpus of 675 parseable LLM tool calls from a smart-lamp test rig (three tools: set_brightness, set_label, reboot), produced by five LLMs (DeepSeek V3, Qwen 2.5-72B-Instruct, Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B, GLM-4-32B, MiniMax-M2.5) responding to 60 adversarial prompts each across 6 attack categories; corpus and generator (tools/gen_llm_corpus.py, tools/llm_corpus.json) shipped in the paper's own repo

AgentDojo's seven prompt-injection attack templates (Direct, IgnorePrevious, SystemMessage, InjecAgent, ImportantInstructions, ToolKnowledge, DoS-flavored variant) -- an external benchmark, adapted (not created) by this paper for the device-control setting

Physical hardware benchmarks: ESP32-WROOM-32 (CH340 USB-UART) and ESP32-S3 / LILYGO T-Panel S3 (native USB-Serial/JTAG) for footprint and latency measurement; 1000 timed calls per transport for latency, 13 round-trip conformance test cases
quantitative_results: Capability escalation: DCP and OpenAPI reject 100% (138/138 reboot calls); Raw MCP and IoT-MCP reject 0%

Prompt injection: DCP rejects 78% (125/160 parsed calls) after v0.3.1 patch, tying OpenAPI; pre-patch DCP rejected only 30% on a 295-call sub-corpus (OpenAPI ~50% on same sub-corpus); MCP-family protocols reject ~1% (2/160)

Per-category rejection rates for DCP across all six attack categories (Figure 3): out-of-range 8%, unit confusion 7%, wrong type 5%, unknown intent 27%, capability escalation 100%, prompt injection 78%

Firmware footprint: DCP layer adds 27.6 KB flash / 0.6 KB RAM on ESP32 (measured by differencing against bare Arduino sketch); full lamp sketch with Arduino runtime + FreeRTOS + DCP = 295 KB flash / 22.7 KB globals
Cross-chip footprint (build-validated only): ESP32-C3 289 KB/13.4 KB, C6 266 KB/14.0 KB, H2 292 KB/14.0 KB, P4 326 KB/22.0 KB, S3 322 KB/22.7 KB, ESP8266 242 KB/28.9 KB
Latency: DCP loopback 0.02 ms; DCP/UART WROOM-32 15.56 ms; DCP/UART ESP32-S3 15.60 ms; IoT-MCP-style wire/UART ESP32-S3 15.59 ms (DCP and IoT-MCP-wire differ by ~4 microseconds, within 0.4 ms stdev)
Wire size: DCP frame 19 bytes (no HMAC) / 35 bytes (+HMAC) for a typical single-parameter call, vs CoAP+CBOR ~30 bytes, MCP JSON-RPC ~180 bytes, OpenAPI/HTTP ~320 bytes (illustrative figure)
RAM comparison: DCP layer 0.6 KB static RAM vs IoT-MCP's reported 74 KB peak memory (external, cited figure, not independently reproduced)
baselines_compared: IoT-MCP (Yang et al., arXiv:2510.01260 -- external prior work; used both as a validator baseline in the adversarial study and as the source of the 74 KB/~205 ms figures cited, not reproduced, from the original paper)
OpenAPI 3 (well-formed spec with explicit minimum/maximum/pattern/OAuth2 scopes) -- included explicitly as an upper-bound reference for schema expressiveness, not as a deployable MCU alternative
